slides.title: Atomic Design Principles
slides.content: Atomic design is a methodology for creating design systems, breaking down user interfaces into their smallest building blocks: atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, and pages. Atoms are basic UI elements like labels, inputs, and buttons that cannot be broken down further without losing functionality. Molecules combine atoms to create simple, functional UI components, such as a search form consisting of a label, input, and a search button. Organisms are more complex, distinct UI sections like headers, footers, or navigational sidebars that group molecules together into meaningful content blocks. By systematically building from the smallest unit upwards, developers ensure modularity, reusability, and architectural consistency throughout the entire application. This structured approach helps maintain a cohesive visual language even as the UI library scales significantly. Atomic design allows design-conscious teams to create a 'source of truth' for UI elements, enabling fast, predictable, and scalable development while dramatically reducing design debt as new features are added iteratively over time.
slides.svgContent: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><rect width='100%' height='100%' fill='#fefce8'/><circle cx='80' cy='100' r='10' fill='#facc15'/><circle cx='180' cy='100' r='20' fill='#facc15'/><circle cx='300' cy='100' r='35' fill='#facc15'/><text x='200' y='180' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#a16207'>Atoms to Organisms (Atomic Design)</text></svg>
studentPrompts: How do atomic design principles accelerate the development of large component libraries?
Describe the relationship between atoms, molecules, and organisms in a dashboard UI.
Why is atomic design superior to monolithic component creation for long-term scalability?

slides.title: Data Visualization & User Experience
slides.content: Data visualization bridges the gap between raw data and actionable user insights by transforming complex datasets into meaningful, interactive visual representations. Effective visualization is not just about choosing the right chart (bar, line, scatter-plot, or heatmap) but about maximizing the semantic density of data, enabling quick pattern recognition through intuitive design. Technologies like Recharts or D3.js allow for the creation of high-performance, dynamic charts that respond interactively to user input—zooming, filtering, and hovering to highlight details. A key goal for UI engineers is ensuring visualizations are accessible, performant, and responsive; they must degrade gracefully on mobile and handle large dataset rendering without blocking the main browser thread. Beyond aesthetic appeal, visualization must reinforce the overall application narrative—showing relevant performance metrics, trends, or project states clearly. By thoughtfully applying visualization techniques, developers empower users to make informed, data-driven decisions while keeping the interface clean and contextually relevant throughout their browsing experience.

studentPrompts: When should you choose a scatter plot over a bar chart for categorical data?
What are the performance implications of rendering large datasets using D3 inside the browser DOM?
How can you ensure data visualizations remain accessible to screen readers?
